articles.title: AI Translation, Culture Formation, and the Risk of Cultural Separation: A Conceptual Framework with Propositions
articles.abstract: Language is foundational to culture formation, yet the consequences of AI-mediated translation for intercultural understanding remain insufficiently theorized. This conceptual paper examines whether AI-based translation connects cultures or, under specific conditions, contributes to their separation. Drawing on functionalist, conflict, symbolic interactionist, cultural transmission, and cultural evolution perspectives, the paper reconstructs culture as an emergent social process constituted through co-presence, repeated interaction, norm formation, identity construction, and temporal transmission. On this basis, it develops a five-step model of culture formation and derives five propositions concerning AI translation as socio-technical mediation. The central argument is that AI translation can connect cultures at the level of informational access while, under substitute-use conditions, weakening deeper meaning-based understanding that depends on interpretive effort and linguistic engagement. To capture this tension, the paper introduces the concept of cultural thinning, defined as broader cross-linguistic access combined with reduced depth and quality of shared meaning formation. The analysis further argues that translation systems are not neutral intermediaries: structural biases relating to gender, low-resource languages, and cultural context may reshape intercultural perception in systematically distorted ways, producing cultural misrecognition rather than mere neutral error. Twitter/X is discussed as an illustrative case of platform-mediated intercultural communication under compressed, rapid, and context-sensitive conditions. The paper concludes by outlining an empirical research agenda for examining overconfidence, interpretive loss, bias, and platform effects in AI-mediated intercultural communication.

articles.title: Temporal dimensions of audiobook listening among older adults with visual impairment
articles.authors: Signe Lund Mathiesen, Amanda Grenier, Walter Wittich, Bjorn Herrmann
articles.abstract: Reading is a recurring and immersive temporal practice that shapes daily rhythms and identity across the life course. When visual impairment disrupts access to print, established reading routines and familiar temporal structures can be unsettled. This study examines how audiobook listening (re)shapes the temporal organization of everyday life among older adults with visual impairment. Drawing on in-depth interviews with eleven participants, we identify three interrelated temporal dimensions of audiobook listening. Day- and life-course time captures how listening structures daily routines and sustains reading identity across life transitions. Situational time represents how participants negotiate listening by managing attention and affect within daily contexts. Momentary time captures immersive listening as a transportation into story worlds and a suspension of the present. Together, these findings conceptualize audiobook listening as a multi-scalar temporal practice that supports adaptation, reorganizes everyday rhythms, and contributes to well-being after visual impairment.

articles.title: Mental Health First Aid in the workplace: a reflexive thematic analysis of UK workers’ experiences.
articles.authors: Opeyemi Atanda, Eleni Vangeli, Steven D. Brown, patrick callaghan, Graham Durcan, Kerry V Wood, Tim Carter, Nick O'Shea, Sarah White, Paula Reavey
articles.abstract: Background Mental Health First Aid (MHFA) is widely used in workplace settings to improve mental health literacy, reduce stigma, and encourage help-seeking among employees experiencing distress. This paper explores how employees perceive and engage with MHFA within workplace contexts. Methods This qualitative study was embedded as part of a larger clustered randomised controlled trial (Atanda et al, 2020). Qualitative interviews with twenty-four participants purposefully selected from a list of trial survey respondents were conducted. Participants interviewed included employees who were MHFA recipients, mental health first aiders, employees of the organisation where MHFA was introduced, and senior managers involved in implementing MHFA. Reflexive thematic analysis was used to explore and understand employees ‘perceptions of MHFA. Findings: Three main themes and two subthemes were observed: a) Ambiguity concerning the purpose of MHFA, b) Employees’ reluctance to engage due to ambivalent feelings about MHFA support (subthemes: Anticipated Stigma, MHFA not addressing core concerns affecting staff well-being) and c) Risks of disclosure’s interplay with work-based interpersonal relationships. Employees discussed their doubts about the purpose of MHFA in supporting their mental health concerns. Doubts raised by employees appear to have impacted engagement levels. The employees also described concerns about anticipated negative reactions after disclosing their mental health concerns. Good interpersonal relationships among colleagues contributed to disclosure attempts, but participants appeared concerned about the anticipated impact of the disclosure on their careers and other job opportunities. Discussion: This study highlights challenges to integrating Mental Health First Aid (MHFA) into workplaces, noting that prevailing cultures hinder its effectiveness without addressing stigma and work-related mental health issues. It recommends a strategic approach to introduce MHFA, emphasising workplace culture change for better engagement. The findings clarify MHFA's scope, and future research should test its impact against other workplace mental health initiatives.

articles.title: Housing Supply and Racial Integration: Evidence from Building Permits in U.S. Metropolitan Areas
articles.abstract: Debates over housing supply, from NIMBY opposition to YIMBY advocacy, have focused largely on the economic consequences of permitting new housing, but their broader social effects remain underexplored. This article examines whether housing development shapes racial residential segregation. Using panel data on U.S. metropolitan areas from 1990 to 2020, I show that metros permitting more housing experienced larger declines in Black–White segregation. An instrumental variable strategy exploiting geologic constraints on buildability strengthens causal interpretation, and place-level analyses demonstrate that this relationship also holds for towns within metropolitan areas. I further assess several proposed mechanisms, including housing costs, residential mobility, and the location and type of new development, finding no single pathway fully explains the association. Together, these results suggest that housing supply operates as a macro-institutional condition that structures residential sorting. The findings position housing development not only as an economic outcome, but as a consequential social process shaping patterns of racial inequality.

articles.title: Road Violence and Perceptions of Safety among Pedestrians and Cyclists
articles.authors: Jose Pina-Sánchez, Alexander Trinidad, Ian Loader
articles.abstract: This registered report investigates whether involvement in road traffic collisions meaningfully shapes pedestrians’ and cyclists’ perceptions of safety. Drawing on criminological insights - particularly the fear‑victimisation paradox - the study explores the common policy assumption that reducing collisions alone improves subjective safety. Using ESRA2 survey data from 24 European countries, the analysis estimates both average and conditional treatment effects of collision experience on perceived safety, accounting for vulnerability (age, gender) and exposure (active travel frequency). The study employs equivalence testing and counterfactual modelling to assess whether victims’ perceptions differ from non‑victims’. Findings will clarify whether collision reduction strategies are sufficient to encourage active travel or whether broader interventions addressing vulnerability and environmental cues are required.

articles.title: Social Media Addiction and Sleep Duration in Young Adults: Evidence From a Large Public Dataset
articles.abstract: Social media use has become pervasive among young adults, which raises concerns about the impact on mental and physical health. Using a public dataset with a sample of 705 students aged 16–25, containing participants’ age, average hours of sleep per night, and an ordinal 1-10 social media addiction score (derived from the Bergen Social Media Addiction Scale), this study examined three research questions. First, is there a relationship between social media addiction and hours of sleep per night? A linear regression showed that each one-point increase in social media addiction score was associated with a 0.54 hour decrease in average sleep duration, with 59% of the variance accounted for by the model. Second, do suspected addiction levels (Not Likely Addicted, Possibly Addicted, or Likely Addicted) differ significantly in mean sleep duration? A one-way between-subjects ANOVA (and subsequent pairwise comparisons) indicated significant differences in average sleep duration across all three addiction levels. Third, is there a relationship between addiction levels and adequate sleep (as recommended by the Mayo Clinic)? A chi-square test also revealed a strong association between addiction level and sleep adequacy, with the Not Likely Addicted group showing significantly higher rates of adequate sleep and the Likely Addicted group showing significantly higher rates of inadequate sleep. Keywords: social media addiction, behavioral addiction, sleep duration, BSMAS

articles.title: From Awareness to Application: Ethical Understanding Among Interdisciplinary Undergraduate Students
articles.abstract: The rapid advancement of modern science and technology has intensified the need for ethical awareness and responsible decision-making among future professionals. While undergraduate students are often exposed to foundational ethical concepts, the depth of their understanding and their ability to critically evaluate ethical challenges remain unclear. This study presents an exploratory analysis of undergraduate students’ awareness, perceptions, and interpretative understanding of ethical issues in modern science. A structured online survey was conducted with 50 participants from diverse academic backgrounds, examining not only awareness levels but also the perceived adequacy of existing ethical frameworks and the relationship between ethical understanding and trust in scientific practices. The findings indicate that while a majority of students demonstrate baseline awareness of ethical issues, significant uncertainty persists regarding the effectiveness and applicability of current ethical frameworks. Notably, the results reveal a gap between ethical awareness and deeper critical engagement, particularly in relation to emerging domains such as data privacy and technological accountability. This study contributes to the existing literature by highlighting the distinction between awareness and applied ethical understanding among undergraduate students. It underscores the need for more structured, application-oriented ethics education that moves beyond theoretical exposure to practical engagement with real-world ethical dilemmas. As a preliminary investigation, the study provides a foundation for future research on strengthening ethical competencies in scientific education. This study is novel in distinguishing between ethical awareness and applied ethical understanding among undergraduate students, highlighting a critical gap that is often overlooked in existing research.

articles.title: From land prices to construction deficits: Understanding housing overappreciation in Santiago
articles.abstract: During the 2010s, Chile experienced significant housing overvaluation and unaffordability. This chapter challenges the prevailing belief that land costs for developers are the primary cause of this issue. This study analyzes official data from Santiago, the capital city, showing that, on average, land costs for developers did not increase but slightly decreased between 2010 and 2019 due to fewer public restrictions on residential density. This indicates that land costs alone cannot explain the substantial rise in housing prices. This study investigates the impact of other economic and political factors on housing overappreciation, finding a surge in housing demand due to immigration and a shortage of new housing constructed since 2000 as a more probable cause. This supply shortage led to higher housing prices and rents, while housing demand became increasingly inelastic to absorb them. This study explores potential reasons for the lack of new housing construction and highlights the possibility of developers’ collusion, being a shortcoming of Chile’s neoliberal housing market. Finally, this study offers suggestions for improving market competitiveness, boosting new housing construction, and thus improving housing affordability in Chile.

articles.title: An energetic theory of urbanization in ancient societies
articles.abstract: Urbanization is one of the most consequential and best-documented processes in the archaeological and historical record, yet we lack a general quantitative theory to explain it in ancient societies. I address this gap by developing a model of urbanization that integrates the comparative insights of archaeology and history with settlement scaling theory and human macroecology. The model proposes that urbanization---measured as the population size of a polity's largest settlement relative to polity population---is promoted by the scale of political organization and the economic opportunities created by a polity's largest settlement but constrained by agricultural productivity and the costs of transportation. I formalize this model as a system of interrelated scaling relationships that measure these variables and their interactions in terms of the general properties of human social networks and the energetic constraints on all social systems embedded in space. The model predicts that the population size of a polity's largest settlement scales as the two-thirds power of polity population, a prediction that is confirmed in hundreds of ancient societies from across the globe, spanning the last 10,000 years.

articles.title: Gender Differences in Religious Participation during Adolescence: Exploring longitudinal development using genetically informed data
articles.authors: Christoph	 Spörlein, Elmar Schlueter, Josue Teran Linarte
articles.abstract: Gender differences in religious participation are well-documented in Western societies, yet the mechanisms underlying these disparities remain a subject of debate. While psychological accounts emphasize heritable personality traits, sociological perspectives highlight gendered socialization and social control. This study bridges these perspectives by utilizing a genetically informed design to examine how genetic and environmental influences structure religious participation throughout adolescence. Drawing on longitudinal data from the TwinLife panel study (approx. 4,000 twin pairs in Germany), we employ genetic simplex, age-moderated, and bivariate ACE models to track the development of religious participation from age 10 into early adulthood. Our findings reveal that while mean gender differences in participation are modest in the German context, the underlying sources of variation are differ substantially by gender. For girls, shared environmental influences (the family context) remain the primary driver of participation well into the mid-20s, suggesting a prolonged influence of familial social regulation and constrained individual autonomy. In contrast, boys exhibit an earlier shift from shared environmental to genetic and non-shared environmental influences, reflecting a faster transition toward individual religious autonomy. Furthermore, bivariate analyses indicate that the genetic overlap between personality traits (e.g., risk-taking, agreeableness) and religious participation is specific to females, suggesting that personality-based accounts are insufficient to explain gender differences more broadly. These results are consistent with the idea that social environments may moderate the expression of genetic propensities in a gendered manner.

articles.title: Using Layered Scaffolding to Protect the Writing Process in the Age of AI
articles.abstract: Generative AI tools are increasingly present in second language (L2) writing environments, offering learners rapid feedback, revision support, and even the ability to generate extended texts. While these systems can help students produce linguistically polished writing, they also raise developmental concerns when key composing processes such as generating ideas, structuring arguments, and revising language are carried out by the system rather than the learner. This paper conceptualizes this shift as writing-process conflation: the merging of composing activities that have traditionally unfolded over time into a single AI-assisted interaction. In response, I propose the Sequenced–Segmented Layered Scaffolding (SSLS) framework, an instructional architecture designed to preserve the developmental value of writing in AI-assisted contexts. The framework structures writing instruction through three principles: sequencing, which encourages learners to develop reasoning before drafting; segmentation, which makes rhetorical work visible through move-level composing; and layered revision, which stages feedback across linguistic levels to support noticing and active engagement with language. Drawing on research in second language writing, genre pedagogy, scaffolding, and second language acquisition, the paper outlines how SSLS can guide the design of AI-assisted writing instruction that supports learner agency while using AI as a constrained dialogic partner. The framework contributes to ongoing discussions in L2 writing research about how emerging technologies can be integrated without bypassing the cognitive and linguistic processes through which academic writing ability develops.

articles.title: Integrated Bio-Ethical Literacy: A Primary Prevention Model to Augment the POCSO Framework and Eradicate Sexual Predation — An International Comparative Perspective
articles.abstract: Global child protection frameworks — including India's Protection of Children from Sexual Offences (POCSO) Act, 2012, and international counterparts such as the UK Children Act 1989, Germany's Bundeskinderschutzgesetz 2012, South Africa's Children's Act 2005, and the universal standards of the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child (UNCRC, 1989) — share a foundational structural limitation: they are predominantly reactive instruments, intervening only after violation has occurred. Despite stringent penal provisions, the global incidence of child sexual abuse remains a persistent crisis, underscored most recently by the passage of the United States Epstein Files Transparency Act (Public Law 119-38, 2025) and the subsequent release of over 3.5 million pages of DOJ investigative documents revealing the systemic institutional enablement of large-scale child sexual exploitation. This paper proposes the Integrated Bio-Ethical Literacy (IBL) Model, an evidence-based educational framework designed to shift the paradigm from punitive deterrence to primary prevention through the cultivation of Cognitive Immunity in children and future adults. Drawing upon developmental neuroscience, comparative criminology, forensic science, and international child IBL Model | Working Paper | Page 2 protection law, the IBL Model advocates for the systematic integration of neurobiological literacy, grooming-pattern identification, and bodily sovereignty education into K-12 curricula globally. The paper demonstrates that the IBL Model constitutes both a necessary evolution of the POCSO Act's Section 43 awareness mandate and a direct operationalisation of the UNCRC's Article 19 prevention obligations. This paper forms part of a continuing research series on POCSO implementation, forensic science integration, investigation procedures, and comparative international child protection frameworks

articles.title: Does Mandatory Military Service Shape Men's Occupational Choices? Evidence from the Suspension of Conscription Across Europe
articles.abstract: Recent geopolitical tensions have renewed debates on conscription and mandatory military service. While a substantial literature examines its consequences for education, employment, and earnings, less is known about whether military service shapes subsequent occupational placement. This study investigates whether conscription influenced men's early-career placement in the armed forces, in skill- and license-related civilian occupations, and in male-dominated occupations, and additionally explores whether such effects vary across different forms of institutionalized conscription. Drawing on repeated cross-sectional data from the European Labour Force Survey (1995--2024), we exploit the suspension of conscription across 18 European countries to estimate cohort-level effects in a staggered difference-in-differences design. Consistent with descriptive individual-level associations in European SHARE data, the estimates suggest that cohorts no longer exposed to conscription are less likely to work in all three types of occupations. However, the effects are modest in magnitude and statistically imprecise, and analyses of heterogeneity by conscription selectivity and service duration yield no consistent patterns. Overall, the findings contribute to understanding how conscription can shape subsequent socio-economic attainment and provide a first step toward a comparative perspective on how these effects may vary across conscription regimes.

articles.title: From Additive Effects to Household Configurations: Reinterpreting Parental Influence in Intergenerational Occupational Transmission
articles.abstract: Research on intergenerational occupational transmission typically models parental influences as additive and independent, treating maternal and paternal occupations as separable inputs. This study shows that such additive models are algebraically equivalent to configurational specifications that represent parental occupations as joint household configurations. Despite this equivalence, the two approaches are not interpretively equivalent. Drawing on Bourdieu and Passeron’s concept of the household as an “ensemble,” the study advances a configurational perspective in which dual-parent exposure serves as a baseline of joint occupational presence, and mother-only and father-only configurations are deviations from this baseline. Using data from students in 79 countries, the analysis examines occupational alignment in Health and Technical STEM fields. While both specifications yield identical fitted values, the configurational interpretation reveals systematic asymmetries across household configurations that are not directly visible in additive parameterisations. Differences commonly read as parental effects can be re-expressed as contrasts corresponding to the absence of the other parent relative to the dual-parent baseline, varying by gender and field. More broadly, the findings highlight how statistically equivalent models can support substantively different interpretations when the underlying comparison between household configurations is made explicit.

articles.title: Aporofobia Indígena no Brasil
articles.abstract: A situação dos indígenas brasileiros é de exclusão, onde a imensa maioria reside nas reservas a eles destinadas. Este artigo objetiva explorar o conceito de aporofobia em relação ao indígena brasileiro. Os resultados demonstram que a aporofobia indígena emerge como um campo discursivo mensurável, com similaridade de 0,61 entre phobia e poverty no FastText e acima de 0,85 no BERT, revelando uma sobreposição semântica entre índio, pobreza e discriminação. A pesquisa analisou 676 notícias online publicadas ao longo de 12 meses em quatro veículos de comunicação, e utilizou análise léxica e de contexto com métodos FastText e BERT. Os resultados apontam ainda que 76% das notícias tratam dos direitos indígenas, porém foi detectado que a aversão e a pobreza têm comportamentos de grande similaridade. Encontramos ainda que o indígena é mais relacionado aos termos de pobreza que os de hostilidade e aversão. Este artigo contribui para preencher a lacuna sobre estudos de aporophobia no Brasil, e avança no entendimento da percepção de conceitos que moldam a sociedade brasileira contemporânea. Os resultados indicam que a aporofobia indígena emerge como uma região semântica concreta — um subconjunto do discurso social onde pobreza e rejeição se cruzam no campo indígena— oferecendo evidências empíricas que podem orientar políticas públicas voltadas aos povos indígenas e ao enfrentamento do preconceito.

articles.title: When Social Democrats Move Right on Migration, Who Responds? Lessons from Germany's Social Democrats
articles.abstract: Social democratic parties increasingly adopt more restrictive positions on migration in response to radical right challengers, yet we know little about whether such moves actually shift public opinion. We examine this mechanism in Germany, where form chancellor Scholz's October 2023 call to deport people ``more often and faster'' marked a visible break with the SPD's earlier migration profile. Using the fact that the statement occurred during ongoing European Social Survey fieldwork, we compare respondents interviewed just before and just after the intervention. We show that migration quickly became more salient in public debate, but attitudinal effects were limited in scope and duration. Left-leaning respondents and likely SPD supporters remained largely unaffected, while only voters in the political center briefly moved toward more restrictive views, primarily on the economic dimension. These shifts faded quickly and did not translate into greater closeness to the SPD or into sustained changes in government satisfaction overall.

articles.title: Pairing In Situ Surveys and Traces: A Typology for Mobile Linkage Designs
articles.authors: Yifei Lu, Razieh Pourafshari, Lukas Otto, Mareike Wieland, Yung-Ju Chang, Joseph Bayer
articles.abstract: The proliferation of methods for collecting digital traces through mobile devices has produced an increasing range of ways to capture daily behaviors and contexts. Digital traces, and mobile sensors especially, also offer new opportunities for augmenting mobile self-report methods (i.e., ecological momentary assessment; EMA). In this paper, we map the landscape of emergent methods to guide the design of studies that link in analysis – or directly pair in design – mobile traces and intensive surveys (i.e., mobile linkage). We propose a typology for categorizing mobile linkage designs according to two dimensions: timeliness and adaptiveness, which denote the ability of mobile sensing to shape when and what to trigger (in) an EMA survey, respectively. This results in four types of designs: informing, triggering, forming, and adaptive triggering. We then walk through key design considerations, including survey display (e.g., with or without traces), survey length (e.g., single-item vs. 10 items), and survey wording (e.g., past vs. present reference). We conclude by discussing the generalizability and applicability of our typology, as well as the challenges of leveraging the growing toolkit of mobile methods in communication and beyond.

articles.title: Circulatory Propaganda, Narrative Media, and the Rhythms of Digital Conflict
articles.abstract: This paper examines a cluster of AI-generated, Lego-style videos that circulated widely during the March–April 2026 U.S.–Iran conflict and argues that they illuminate an emerging transformation in propaganda under platform conditions. Produced rapidly in response to unfolding events and distributed across X, Telegram, Instagram, TikTok, and YouTube, these short-form videos combined stylised animation, rhythmic music, ridicule, and geopolitical commentary in highly shareable formats. Rather than understanding them primarily as attempts at persuasion or misinformation, the paper proposes the concept of circulatory propaganda to describe political media whose force derives less from belief change than from visibility, repetition, and persistence across digital platforms. Drawing on McLuhan, Baudrillard, and Virilio, it argues that meaning is increasingly stabilised through movement rather than verification, and through recurrence rather than narrative coherence. The paper also examines producer self-descriptions such as Narrative Media and Narratainment, terms offered by representatives associated with Explosive Media who rejected the label of propaganda, suggesting that contemporary influence operations increasingly frame themselves as hybrid forms of entertainment-driven storytelling. Finally, it argues that the metaphor of meme war may now be insufficient, as today’s information environment resembles a synthetic media tsunami: a continuous flood of AI songs, fake battlefield footage, parody leaders, animated conspiracies, and instant meme responses that together reshape how politics is encountered.

articles.title: What is AI?
articles.authors: Marisa Duarte, Stephen Carradini, Sarah Florini, Camilla Fojas, Alex Halavais, Jaime Kirtz, Nicholas Proferes, Michael Simeone, Shawn Walker
articles.abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is a term with an array of contingent and shifting meanings, unified only by a general aspiration to use mathematical computation to emulate human cognition, language, and problem-solving. Rather than a technical term, “AI” can be thought of as a sociotechnical system and assemblage, consisting of technical objects and social imaginaries, practices, and ideologies. How we characterize and conceptualize AI shapes how, why, and when we use it and when we may constrain or refuse its use. This paper provides a primer on various technologies that often fall under the rubric of “AI” and outlines the various discursive and cultural meanings carried by the term.

articles.title: Escaping Flatland: Understanding Multi-Dimensional Potentials of AI Literacies in College Research and Writing
articles.authors: Michael Simeone, Michael Stancliff, Marisa Duarte, Sarah Florini, Alex Halavais, Jaime Kirtz, Nicholas Proferes, Shawn Walker
articles.abstract: The concept of “AI literacy” has become popularized in response to the rise of large language models (LLM) and generative AI tools. Faced with a generation of products that are broadly useful but difficult to understand technically, teachers, researchers, managers, and more have propounded “literacy” as a possible solution. Literacy is a powerful concept across domains: it promotes greater accessibility and use, and if AI is becoming more pervasive and powerful, the reasoning goes, we should certainly have more AI literacy so we can be prepared for it. But when we use literacy to signify merely skills competency, we fall into problems that have haunted the concept for decades.

articles.title: Actor-network storytelling: Narrative-as-a-network and the gacha game model in Genshin Impact
articles.abstract: This article proposes the actor-network theory (ANT) as a framework to analyze the narrative experience of games-as-a-service (GaaS) with a focus on gacha games. Although narrative is central to both player enjoyment and marketing strategies in gacha games, it remains theoretically neglected because its dispersed and amorphous narrative experience is incompatible with traditional models that posit the narrative text as a unified, self-contained entity. Drawing on Law’s conceptualization of ANT and Latour’s exploration of “beings of fiction,” this article develops an alternative model of ‘actor-network storytelling.’ This model approaches the video game narrative as a network produced by complex interplays among heterogeneous human and non-human actors both within and outside the game world, including players’ interpretations and the developer’s policy. Applying this framework to a case study of Genshin Impact (2020), the article demonstrates how the game provides a fragmentary and porous network of narrative information to enroll individual players and collective fandom into the process of completing, personalizing, and enhancing the story. While this strategy enables a diverse and enriched narrative network, it simultaneously renders the storytelling volatile and fragile, threatened by discursive friction and a lack of interiority. By introducing this relational framework, the article contributes to the academic pursuit of alternative storytelling models for interactive media and offers new insights into emerging forms of digital narrative.

articles.title: The Morality of Petty Corruption in the UK
articles.abstract: Why do citizens engage with and tolerate petty bribery and favouritism so differently? Research on petty corruption has focused on developing countries and treated different practices as equivalent. Drawing on moral and social psychology to complement existing accounts centred on social norms and institutional constraints, this paper argues that variation in citizen engagement can also be explained by the activation of distinct moral domains: both types of corruption violate fairness norms, but favouritism additionally invokes loyalty and kinship obligations, making it morally defensible. I test this through a vignette experiment in the UK, where impartiality expectations reduce confounding, crossing corruption type (favouritism, bribery) with signalled social norms (pro- or anti-corruption). Participants reported willingness to engage, perceived corruption and illegality, and justified their reasoning in open responses analysed via LLM classification and the eMFD. Corruption type consistently outweighed norm effects, and favouritism elicited steeper rationalisation and more loyalty language.

articles.title: CAL FIRE and Intersectional Inequality: Gender, LGBTQIA2S+ and the Governance of Incarcerated Labor
articles.abstract: This article examines California’s CAL FIRE conservation camps as a contemporary system of coerced labor enabled by the 13th Amendment’s punishment clause. While the state publicly frames incarcerated firefighting as voluntary rehabilitation, document analysis reveals that participation is structured through coercive conditions, disciplinary threats, and economic incentives that mirror historical systems of racialized forced labor such as Black Codes and convict leasing. Drawing on state documents, CDCR policies, CAL FIRE training materials, legislative hearings, audits, and media reports, this study demonstrates how gender and sexuality shape access to labor assignments within the camps. Women and LGBTQIA2S+ incarcerated people are systematically excluded from masculinized, higher‑status firefighting roles that offer greater pay and double sentence‑reduction credits, and are instead funneled into feminized, low‑wage, surveillance‑heavy work. Using frameworks from racial capitalism, feminist criminology, queer criminology, gendered organizations theory, and intersectionality, the analysis shows how CAL FIRE reproduces patriarchal and heteronormative labor hierarchies inside a carceral setting. Findings reveal that the state’s economic dependence on incarcerated firefighters—saving an estimated $100 million annually—reinforces a labor regime in which coercion, discipline, and inequality are embedded in institutional design. This article argues that CAL FIRE conservation camps represent a modern iteration of coerced labor that extends the historical lineage of racialized and gendered exploitation in the post‑Emancipation United States, and highlights the need to confront the constitutional and economic structures that sustain this system.

articles.title: Feet without stress: High vowel deletion in Québec French
articles.authors: Natália Brambatti Guzzo, Heather Goad, Guilherme Duarte Garcia
articles.abstract: This paper investigates whether high vowel deletion (HVD) in Québec French is conditioned by prosodic structure. In Prosodic Phonology, prosodic constituents are typically assumed to be universal. However, the universality of certain constituents, such as the foot, has been questioned for languages like French, where prominence is only obligatory at the phrase level. We probe this issue by analysing HVD, a lenition process that is sensitive to prominence. We conducted two experiments. Experiment 1 examined the role of phrasal tone profile in HVD, while Experiment 2 examined whether word-internal constituency affects HVD. The results show that, while tone profile does not affect HVD, the process is preferred in dependent positions of iambic feet in even-parity forms. Odd-parity forms show no preference regarding HVD, suggesting that evenand odd-parity forms are parsed differently in Québec French.

articles.title: Intersecting inequalities in later life: work-family trajectories and old age poverty among natives and immigrants in Finland
articles.authors: Maria Vaalavuo, Aart-Jan Riekhoff, Aleksi Karhula
articles.abstract: Concurrently to population ageing, the number of older people with immigrant background is rising. Immigrant background in conjunction with family and labour market trajectories during working age will increasingly shape inequality in late life. In this study, we ask: 1) What kinds of work-family trajectories can be identified among immigrant and native men and women in the years preceding retirement? 2) How do clusters of these trajectories differ by immigration background and gender? 3) How are these clusters linked to socioeconomic outcomes at age 65 and throughout working age? We use Finnish full-population register data from 1987-2022. Our analysis sample consists of individuals observed in the data for the entire observation period from age 45 to 65. Using multichannel sequence analysis and cluster analysis, we identified seven clusters. High-income earner with family was the most common among natives (21%), while low-income earner with family was the most common among immigrants (26%). The identified clusters correlate with differences in earnings development from age 45 to 65 and poverty status at age 65. Poverty at age 65 is higher among immigrants in all clusters, while being single is an important predictor of poverty regardless of the cluster. Gender appeared to affect poverty and earnings development much less than immigrant background when work-life trajectory was taken into account. Our study provides novel evidence on older immigrants and illustrates great heterogeneity regarding work-family trajectories, highlighting the importance of analysing determinants of poverty and inequality across the life course in a heterogenous population.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Is research performance related to academic freedom? A large-scale empirical analysis on the national level
articles.abstract: A robust science system is crucial for national prosperity. Robert K. Merton, the pioneer of modern sociology of science, outlined the ethos of science with four key norms: disinterestedness, communalism, universalism, and organized skepticism. The continuous generation of scientific knowledge is believed to rely on the effective functioning of these norms. To explore the relationship between adherence to the science ethos and national knowledge production, this study examines the correlation between an index measuring national academic freedom (AF) and bibliometric indicators, such as publication counts. We applied three different identification strategies, namely two-way fixed effects regressions, within-between random effects regression, and difference-in-differences estimators, and used different bibliometric indicators to calculate the effect of AF on national performance. We found a positive and statistically significant effect of the ‘between AF’ variance on bibliometric indicators with a pooled effect size of 1.5128 (95%-CI = 1.2762; 1.7494]). ‘within AF’ has a positive pooled effect size of 0.1654 (95%-CI = [0.1149; 0.2158]), and is statistically significant at conventional levels. These results can be summarized as follows: Whereas differences between countries in enabling AF are related to differences in their national performance, changes in enabling AF within a country are also reflected in annual performance differences.

articles.title: Initiatives and Challenges of Agricultural Cooperatives in Advancing the Sustainable Development Goals: Evidence from Region I, Philippines
articles.authors: Solomon Tablang, Josefina Bitonio, RALPH RENDELL TOLEDO
articles.abstract: Agricultural cooperatives are widely recognized as important institutional actors in advancing the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), particularly in agrarian and rural economies. This study examines the initiatives, services, and constraints of agricultural cooperatives in relation to the SDGs in Region I of the Philippines. A mixed-methods design was used, combining a structured survey of officers from 30 medium and large agricultural cooperatives with selected semi-structured interviews to provide context for the quantitative findings. Descriptive statistics were employed to summarize cooperative profiles, services, and SDG-aligned practices, while cross-tabulations were used to examine differences in governance, climate- and resource-related, and market challenges by cooperative size. The findings show that most cooperatives are medium-sized and long-established, with lean staffing structures and moderate asset bases. Core services remain concentrated on credit provision and commodity distribution, while higher-value activities such as processing and mechanization are less common. Cooperatives implement a wide range of initiatives aligned with multiple SDGs, with the strongest engagement observed in poverty reduction, food security, decent work, and partnerships. These contributions, however, are constrained by persistent challenges related to governance, limited access to finance, market competition, and environmental and climate risks. Medium-sized cooperatives report greater vulnerabilities in governance, environmental pressures, and market access than larger cooperatives, which, despite greater resilience, face more pronounced bureaucratic constraints. Overall, the results point to the need for profile-sensitive interventions that strengthen governance, expand access to finance and markets, and improve the integration of environmental sustainability into cooperative operations. Addressing these areas can enhance the capacity of agricultural cooperatives to contribute more effectively to inclusive and sustainable rural development and to the achievement of the SDGs.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Provocative Storytelling: Narrative, Interpretation, and the Problem of Meaning in Pedagogy
articles.abstract: Storytelling is widely used in education, but it is still most often treated as a tool for engagement, illustration, or representation rather than as a structure for thought. I theorize provocative storytelling as a pedagogical construct that positions narrative as a site of inquiry. Rather than delivering meaning, provocative stories are deliberately designed to generate interpretive tension, inviting learners into processes of questioning, perspective-taking, and dialogic reasoning. Drawing on narrative theory, dialogism, critical literacy, and inquiry-based learning, the article articulates the design principles that distinguish provocative storytelling from adjacent approaches such as case-based learning and dilemma pedagogy. It argues that contemporary pedagogical practices often privilege coherence, clarity, and transmission at the expense of interpretive engagement, limiting opportunities for students to grapple with complexity, ambiguity, and competing perspectives (Biesta, 2010). By framing narrative as an epistemic space rather than a rhetorical device, this article contributes a theoretically grounded approach to pedagogy that foregrounds meaning-making as a social, cultural, and interpretive process. The article concludes with implications for curriculum design, teacher education, and classroom practice, particularly in contexts that seek to engage learners in critical, interdisciplinary, and culturally responsive inquiry.

articles.title: The Power to Control Our Own Destiny: Neurodiversity, Epistemic Authority, and the Significance of Autistic Researchers
articles.abstract: Within conversations regarding autism, the autistic community is frequently denied epistemic authority due to the assumption that autism impairs our capacity to accurately understand our own experiences. Although non-autistic practitioners claim their frameworks can produce objective science independent of their biases, autism research continues to reinforce dehumanizing conceptualizations of autism, leading to marginalization and widespread harm. This dominance makes it paramount to explore autism research from the vantage point of autistic-led research. Utilizing my autistic experience, I argue that we bring unique contributions that facilitate compassionate, ethical, and robust autism research centered in neurodiversity that challenges the status quo. Our narratives detailing personal hopes, struggles, and triumphs serve to demonstrate our epistemic authority and contributory expertise, and this expertise can counter ableist autism frameworks while uplifting essential subjects into the scientific realm such as masking, autistic burnout, stimming, therapeutic goals and institutional reform. Crucially, in contrast to non-autistic-led fields like applied behavior analysis, our methodologies of community-based participatory research and reflexivity are derived from our status as a minority community, embracing diversity, subjectivity, social change, and self-determination in the hopes of emancipating all autistic people from oppression, and therefore, we deserve the spotlight to lead this field forward.

articles.title: Stratification of the Scientific Workforce in Global Forest Research: Configurations of Capital, Gender and Geography
articles.authors: Isabel Basson, Nelius Boshoff, Jonathan Dudek, Rodrigo Costas, Shizuku Sunagawa, Susanne Koch
articles.abstract: Drawing on a Bourdieusian understanding of science, our study examines how the global forest research workforce is stratified by configurations of capital, gender and geography. We studied four capital types, representing both scientific and social capital: scientific output (papers), symbolic recognition (citations), national collaborative ties (national co-authors), and international collaborative ties (international co-authors). Our dataset, compiled from Dimensions, contains author-level data on these capital types for 125,501 researchers who have published in forestry sciences between 2005 and 2024. Using Latent Profile Analysis (LPA), a multivariate, person-centered statistical approach, we inductively identified seven subgroups of researchers. The smallest subgroup (3.3%) dominates the field at all capital levels, while two other subgroups, which are considered ‘subordinate’ in the Bourdieusian sense, together represent half (50.4%) of all forest researchers. We measured gender in terms of masculine or feminine author names and geography as the region of academic origin, based on the country of affiliation of the first paper. Our intersectional analysis of gender, region, and subgroup reveals that, although women and researchers from the Global South are present in forest research and contribute to it, they remain disproportionately concentrated in subordinate subgroups.

articles.title: Five Years After VMT Reform: Project Siting Before and After SB 743
articles.abstract: This report provides a comparative analysis of development project siting in the City of San Mateo before and after the implementation of Vehicle Miles Traveled (VMT) policies mandated by California Senate Bill 743 (SB 743) in 2020. The purpose of this comparison is to determine whether or not the policy changes have successfully influenced new projects to be located in more VMT-efficient areas, which is a core objective of the policy. This research used archived lists of development projects provided on the City of San Mateo’s website to construct a pair of two-year samples to represent pre- and post-SB 743 projects. The C/CAG VMT Screening Tool was used to test each project to determine if the location was in a Transit Priority Area and/or a Low-VMT Area, two commonly used metrics in VMT analysis. The research found that, following the implementation of SB 743 requirements, the proportion of projects in Transit Priority Areas increased from 69.4% to 80.0%, and among approved projects, increased from 58.8% to 87.5%. However, the proportion of projects located in Low-VMT Areas did not show the same patterns. The results suggest that SB 743 is effectively encouraging development near transit, but falls short in steering projects to the most VMT-efficient areas, potentially limiting its emissions-reduction impact. The steering of projects towards TPAs may be the result of multiple overlapping incentives. This paper is intended for planners and policymakers evaluating the on-the-ground effects of SB 743.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Total Propaganda: A Paving-Over
articles.abstract: One of the key questions before sociologists and political scientists of the current day is how propaganda works. It's well agreed that propaganda works on other people, and most will accept that they are themselves susceptible to propaganda, but no one wants to admit that propaganda does actually work on them and, therefore, there is little understanding of how it works in the particular. This is one of the central motivating questions of Jacques Ellul's Propagandes (published in English as Propaganda: The Formation of Men’s Attitudes). As one of the more thorough explorations of propaganda available in English, at least of those who purport to serious and wholistic analysis, it seems to be a good place to begin when attempting to learn more about the subject. His answer is the concept of “total propaganda”, an all-consuming, surrounding propaganda force that pushes people into actions against their conscious will. Unfortunately, Ellul's scholarship is sloppy, frequently dishonest, and clearly pre-determined in a way that shapes his use of evidence rather than his ideas being shaped by the evidence he has. The purpose of this commentary on the first chapter of Ellul's Propagandes is twofold. First, I want to draw out the mistakes in Ellul's work in a more thorough way than is possible in a two-page book review so that I can support these misgivings. Second, I want to develop a new concept to counter Ellul's idea of "total propaganda". This new concept is the linked (but distinct) propaganda campaign-machine.

articles.title: A Handmaid’s Tale: The Reproductive Bias in OB-GYN Science
articles.authors: Netta Avnoon, Shunit Agmon, Plia Gillis
articles.abstract: Feminist research has long argued that obstetrics and gynecological (OB-GYN) science and practice are biased toward reproduction. This bias means that women’s health is understudied in OB-GYN and is often not the primary focus of this medical specialty; instead, successful reproduction is prioritized, and women as patients are reduced to their reproductive roles. To quantify this bias, we applied a deep learning topic model (BERTopic) to 305,063 PubMed abstracts published in 83 journals under the category OBSTETRICS & GYNECOLOGY in the Journal Citation Reports (JCR), extracting over 2,500 topics. We then labeled the 1166 most prevalent topics and assigned each a topic category. Results show that 61% of OB-GYN articles focus solely on women’s reproductive roles. An additional 16% focus on gynecological cancer. All other women’s health issues make up only 23% of OB-GYN research. We discuss the underlying factors contributing to this Handmaid’s Tale in its OB-GYN version, including historical and ongoing gender discrimination in healthcare and persistent vertical gender segregation within the OB-GYN profession. We then propose some routes for change.

articles.title: Institutional Mapping of Gender Marginalization in Indonesia’s Multi-Level Climate and Fisheries Governance
articles.authors: Tyas Ismi Trialfhianty, Stanislaus Risadi Apresian, Rahmad Supriyanto, Helen Fox, Maria Beger
articles.abstract: As climate change intensifies, the integration of gender perspectives into climate adaptation and fisheries governance becomes increasingly critical to ensure equitable and effective policy outcomes. In Indonesia, where coastal communities are highly vulnerable, institutional coordination plays a pivotal role in shaping inclusive and climate-resilient development. This study investigates the institutional landscape of gender integration within climate adaptation and fisheries governance in Indonesia through an in-depth institutional mapping analysis. It examines the interactions among national ministries, regional governments, and local authorities, identifying structural fragmentation, overlapping mandates, and coordination gaps that hinder the effective mainstreaming of gender considerations. Although gender and climate agendas are articulated at the national level, the mapping reveals limited translation into subnational policies and practices. Furthermore, gender-focused institutions demonstrate weak linkages within the broader governance architecture, constraining their influence on policy formulation and implementation. The analysis highlights the critical need for multi-level coordination, enhanced inter-agency collaboration, and institutional reform to support inclusive, gender-responsive, and climate-resilient fisheries governance. The findings offer evidence-based recommendations to strengthen policy coherence and promote equity in the climate adaptation of coastal and marine resources.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Interpreting inequalities and causal considerations – reflections on the application of descriptive intersectional MAIHDA
articles.authors: Natalie Bennett, Andrew Bell, Gareth Griffith, George Leckie, Clare Evans
articles.abstract: Intersectional MAIHDA (multilevel analysis of individual heterogeneity and discriminatory accuracy) is a novel method for analysing intersectional inequalities that is increasingly popular in health-related research. The method is characterised by its unusual application of a standard multilevel modelling framework, which allows users to describe inequalities in an outcome across multiple intersecting characteristics simultaneously. It does this by nesting individuals within the relevant combination of intersecting characteristics, or ‘strata’. However, the aims of such analyses are not always clear, and misunderstandings of the fundamental goals of intersectional MAIHDA are evident. Intersectional MAIHDA and its closely related approaches (including multicategorical MAIHDA and Eco-Intersectional Multilevel models) were initially conceived as descriptive methods, yet some recent applications stray into causal imaginings. Care is needed when designing, conducting and interpreting the results of MAIHDA. There are also several notable risks for analyses situated in the grey area between description and causal inference. In this study, we consider three key issues that warrant attention: (1) how to interpret the coefficients of stratum-defining variables, (2) the potential for inadvertent collider adjustment and generalisability bias, illustrated using simulated data, and (3) the inclusion of ‘control’ variables. We provide recommendations for producing more robust descriptive research using MAIHDA.

articles.title: Belonging among the Spiritual but not Religious: Belonging as a valued and sought after affective state for spiritual but not religious residents of Glastonbury UK.
articles.authors: Kathryn D. Ford, Sotirios Kolios, Lora Adair, Matthew M. Gervais, Aiyana Willard, Michelle Kline
articles.abstract: Spiritual but not religious beliefs are often characterised by hyper individualistic beliefs without community. We suggest that the quest for a sense of belonging and likeminded community remains important to spiritual seekers. We detail findings from 30 semi-structured interviews conducted with people who are spiritual but not religious and live near to or in Glastonbury UK, a small town with a reputation as a New Age pilgrimage site and an area of high spiritual diversity. Thematic analysis conducted on transcripts of participant interviews highlighted the importance of affective belonging to participants. Participants were motivated to leave social contexts where they experienced a lack of affective belonging and many participants invested time and effort into finding sources of affective belonging and creating sources of affective belonging for others. This paper challenges the notion that spiritual but not religious individuals are un-interested in sources of belonging and suggest that future work into the role of community among the spiritual but not religious should focus on affective belonging rather than group labels.

articles.title: CALIBRATED AI REVIEWER: AN ALGORITHM FOR COMPARING SCIENTIFIC JOURNALS WITH HIGH AND LOW ENTRY BARRIERS
articles.abstract: Traditional journal ranking systems measure citations, not publication quality. Particular attention deserves journals with a low entry barrier that offer virtually guaranteed publication. This raises the question of the actual quality of publications in such journals. Objective – To test an algorithm for comparing scientific journals with high and low entry barriers based on a calibrated AI reviewer. Methodology. A two-level architecture is proposed. At the first level, a random sample of 7 articles is drawn from the latest issue of each journal and independently evaluated by the AI reviewer (10 runs). The strongest, median, and weakest positions are identified. At the second level, competitive pairwise comparison of peer articles is conducted within a single context. The algorithm was tested on two technical journals contrasting in entry barriers. Results. Isolated evaluations create a false impression of comparability between journals (a calibration artifact). Competitive comparison revealed a progressive gap: for the strongest articles, the low-barrier journal lagged by 14.2 points (17%); for median articles – by 26.9 points (42%); for weakest articles – by 27.2 points (54%). Conclusion. The proposed algorithm allows researchers and editors to independently compare journals by actual publication quality rather than by indirect bibliometric indicators. The method is reproducible, requires no subscription to commercial databases, and can be applied to any subject area.

articles.title: Economic Diplomacy for Pakistan
articles.abstract: This paper evaluates the structure and efficacy of Pakistan’s economic diplomacy, contending that although the concept has long been acknowledged at the policy level, its practical realization remains constrained by institutional fragmentation and persistent shortcomings in the domestic economy itself . In contrast to more integrated models (e.g. Canada & Australia), Pakistan’s approach is diffused across multiple ministries with overlapping mandates and limited coordination. The analysis underscores how bureaucratic competition, insufficient specialization in commercial diplomacy, and enduring structural impediments (ranging from regulatory complexity to political instability) diminish external economic engagement. It concludes that a more effective approach necessitates closer alignment between foreign policy and domestic economic reform, supported by institutional coherence and professional capacity.

articles.title: AI Writing Detection in Higher Education: Population-Differentiated False Positives, Statistical Convergence, and the Sociology of Algorithmic Classification
articles.abstract: AI writing detectors are deployed across more than 20,000 educational institutions worldwide and have processed over 250 million submissions. A consistent literature documents systematic false-positive rates concentrated on three populations: non-native English writers, neurodivergent writers, and writers in formal genres whose conventions (medical abstracts, legal briefs, scientific methods sections) produce prose with low burstiness and restricted lexical variety. Drawing on the Stanford TOEFL study, the Rashidi et al. NEJM-abstract findings, the August 2025 Jabarian–Imas Becker Friedman Institute audit, and the broader comparative literature, this paper argues that these failures are not measurement error in an instrument on its way to being improved, but the operational signature of what the instrument is actually doing. The paper develops three claims. First, corpus-separation classifiers report training-distribution proximity, not authorship, and on the populations whose prose sits closest to the training distribution this distinction generates the documented failure pattern. Second, the four families of detection — corpus-separation, paired-discrimination, behavioural-provenance, and watermark — each escape one axis of failure by introducing another, and none addresses the structural convergence between human and machine writing in the formal genres of expository culture. Third, the persistence of the apparatus across institutions that have audited its failures is best read through the sociology of classification: through Hacking's looping kinds, the Wittgenstein–Taleb problem of opaque rulers, and the structural pattern by which classificatory machinery is brought in to defend a metaphysics whose ground has begun to dissolve. The paper contributes to science and technology studies, the sociology of algorithmic classification, and the literature on institutional persistence under demonstrated unreliability.

articles.title: The Climate Conflict Vulnerability Index: Mapping Global (Co-)occurrence of Climate and Conflict Risk
articles.authors: Daniel Mittermaier, Simon Merschroth, Cornelia Auer, Tobias Bohne, Stefano Ferri, Vanessa Gottwick, Sidney Michelini, Sana Slouma, Daniel Racek, Barbora Šedová
articles.abstract: Climate and conflict hazards both threaten human security, and where they coincide their effects may compound. Yet global risk assessments often lack transparency and do not clearly distinguish between climate risk, conflict risk, and the vulnerabilities that shape both. We introduce the Climate Conflict Vulnerability Index (CCVI) to assess where, to what extent, and under which conditions climate and conflict risks (co-)occur. The CCVI is a global index at 0.5° x 0.5° spatial resolution and updated quarterly and harmonizes a wide range of indicators based on publicly available data to create measures of climate and conflict risk together with a combined score identifying places exposed to both. Applying the index globally, we find that combined climate-conflict risk is generally low to moderate, but highly concentrated in specific regions, with the highest levels observed in Africa between the Sahel belt and the equator. We further show that climate and conflict risks are spatially correlated in an asymmetric way: high conflict risk rarely occurs where climate risk is low, whereas high climate risk occurs across the full range of conflict risk. By separating shared vulnerability from climate and conflict hazards, the CCVI helps identify location-specific risk profiles. It constitutes an openly accessible, transparent tool for comparing climate-conflict risk patterns across sub-national regions worldwide.

articles.title: Query Authorship: A Framework for Systematic AI Use in Social Science
articles.abstract: The intellectual contribution in AI-assisted research is not in the text the AI produces but in the instructions that shaped it. Unsystematic use of AI is unaccountable, with a possible consequence of overflowing the scientific literature with noise. In contrast: systematic use of AI is embedded in explicit criteria, subject to human verification, and producing documented outputs. Both modes of AI use are currently treated as equal — but they are not. I introduce the concept of "query authorship". Systematic AI authorship is a necessary condition for epistemic soundness, not a sufficient one: it makes errors visible and correctable rather than eliminating them. It instantiates the properties of open-science practice — externalising tacit commitments, producing verifiable artefacts, consistent with replication package norms. A documentation-based framework that operationalises this distinction is more epistemically honest than binary approaches and requires no new infrastructure.

articles.title: The Manufactured Cost of the Boriswave
articles.abstract: In early 2026, Reform UK published The Cost of the Boriswave, projecting that post-Brexit migration will impose a lifetime fiscal burden of £622 billion, equivalent to approximately £20,000 per British household. The report has been refuted by economists (Gallagher, 2026), but its findings still continue to be used as a key document for Reform’s campaign to ‘stop the Boriswave’. This paper shifts the focus from methodological inquiry to two underexamined dimensions: the ideological function of the term “Boriswave” itself, and the structural, legal, and practical impossibility of the policy remedies Reform UK proposes. The paper concludes that the report is best understood as a knowingly dishonest instrument of political mobilisation, modelled on a Trumpian politics of permanent crisis, that cynically exploits public anxiety while promising a fantasy of control. Keywords: Boriswave; Reform UK; immigration; fiscal policy; Indefinite Leave to Remain; populism; national identity; European Convention on Human Rights

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Zonas de abandono institucional: fragmentação dos mecanismos de resposta à violência doméstica perpetrada por policiais militares no Pará
articles.authors: Heyder Silva do Nascimento, Adrilayne dos Reis Araújo, Edson Marcos Leal Soares Ramos
articles.abstract: A violência doméstica perpetrada por policiais militares (OIDV — Officer-Involved Domestic Violence) constitui fenômeno de alta complexidade institucional, caracterizado por mecanismos de filtragem que reduzem a visibilidade dos casos e fragmentam a resposta institucional. O presente artigo analisa a fragmentação dos mecanismos de resposta à violência doméstica perpetrada por policiais militares no estado do Pará, com base em dados censitários extraídos de cinco bancos de dados institucionais da Polícia Militar do Pará (PMPA), cobrindo o período de 2021 a 2023. Os resultados evidenciam a existência de zonas de abandono institucional — conceito original desenvolvido neste estudo — que descrevem regiões do fluxo institucional onde as vítimas perdem contato com a rede de proteção. Propõe-se um Procedimento Operacional Padrão (POP 023.006) e uma Normativa Institucional para automatizar a prestação de contas e reduzir a subnotificação. Publicado na Veredas do Direito, v. 23, n. 5, 2026. DOI: https://doi.org/10.18623/rvd.v23.5736 Domestic violence perpetrated by military police officers (OIDV — Officer-Involved Domestic Violence) constitutes a highly complex institutional phenomenon, characterized by filtering mechanisms that reduce case visibility and fragment institutional response. This article analyzes the fragmentation of response mechanisms to domestic violence perpetrated by military police officers in the state of Pará, Brazil, based on census data extracted from five institutional databases of the Pará Military Police (PMPA), covering the period 2021–2023. Results evidence the existence of institutional abandonment zones — an original concept developed in this study — describing regions of the institutional flow where victims lose contact with the protection network. A Standard Operating Procedure (SOP 023.006) and an Institutional Normative are proposed to automate accountability and reduce underreporting. Published in Veredas do Direito, v. 23, n. 5, 2026. DOI: https://doi.org/10.18623/rvd.v23.5736

articles.title: Causal Inference: Man or Machine?
articles.abstract: This manuscript is a talk delivered at a symposium in honor of the career of Christopher Winship. It weaves together three stories in order to forecast how machines and artificial intelligence will help us to combat mechanism warlordism and advance the frontiers of knowledge.

articles.title: Blurring evidence with advocacy: A systematic review of policy recommendations for net zero
articles.authors: Evangelos Danopoulos, Aarushi Shah, Claudia R. Schneider, John Aston
articles.abstract: Policy recommendations (PRs) now play a crucial role in tackling the climate crisis, underscoring the need for evidence-based, objective and transparent communication. Scientific studies often put forward PRs but their quality has not yet been evaluated. Here we systematically appraised the quality of PRs in areas of green energy and transportation for tackling climate change. Four databases were searched from 2019. Over three thousand papers were considered and 23 studies are included. Quality rating, based on the Evidence Communication Rules for Policy (ECR-P) critical appraisal tool, indicated poor quality in PRs across all studies. There was a clear trend towards advocacy as opposed to providing neutral information, further exacerbated by inadequate disclosure of uncertainties. Communication quality was markedly better regarding study findings and conclusions compared to PRs. Researchers should use the same scientific rigour and reporting standards in PRs as in any other aspect of their research. A reporting guideline for scientific-based PRs could greatly assist in this area, elevating the standards of communication for both academic publishing and global policymaking while reducing potential criticism that scientists simply put forward their own opinions.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Assessing the Quality of Linked Data
articles.abstract: Linked data is widely used to answer research questions in situations when a single data source does not provide all the information a researcher needs. For linked data to work, the quality of the combined data needs to be sufficient. Naive users of linked data may often treat the linked data as "perfect" and apply standard analysis on it. This may, in the end, lead to biased inference. For example, if we are interested in the relation between age and income, and use linked data in which individuals with higher levels of income are more likely to be linked, we may overestimate the average income in the linked dataset, and also risk biased relations between age and income. In this paper, we review data quality issues of linked data by introducing a general framework for errors in linked datasets. Our framework aims to help linked data users conceptualize potential problems in their studies. It also provides an overview of existing methods that can potentially detect bias and improve the quality of inference. Three examples from the humanities and social sciences are used to illustrate common problems, and some practical suggestions are provided.

articles.title: Do Changing Climate Attitudes Undermine or Reinforce Political Support Among Youth? Evidence from a Salience Shift in Germany
articles.abstract: Can unmet climate demands erode political support among younger citizens? This study examines how rising pro-climate preferences affect political support under conditions of heightened issue salience. The central argument is that policy preferences shape evaluations of incumbent and system performances, primarily when the issue is sufficiently salient to enter citizens' political calculus. Salience functions as a priority weight that activates the link between preference (in)congruence and political support, while shifts in public attention may amplify this relationship over time. Using panel data from the German Longitudinal Election Study (2016--2022), I analyse within-individual preference change and variation in climate salience across cohorts and time. I leverage the rise of Fridays for Future (FFF) as a focal attention environment shift. The results show that climate change carries greater salience for younger than for older citizens; this gap widens following the rise of FFF. Consistent with the activation argument, within individuals, shifts toward more pro-climate preferences are associated with declines in specific support among younger respondents, but less consistently so for older respondents. The link between climate preferences and political support does not strengthen in the post-2019 period. These findings suggest that while climate considerations are more consequential for political evaluations among younger citizens, temporal increases in salience alone are not sufficient to intensify dissatisfaction. More broadly, the results highlight that the political consequences of policy preferences depend on the salience of the issue and the conditions under which attention is translated into evaluative judgements.

articles.title: Patterns of Dietary Habit among Urban Bangladeshi Adults and Its Association with Co-morbidities after COVID-19 Awareness: A Cross-Sectional Study
articles.abstract: Objective: This cross-sectional study aimed to investigate dietary changes among adults in urban areas of Dhaka and Sylhet, Bangladesh, following increased awareness of COVID-19. Methods: The study was conducted in the urban areas of Dhaka and Sylhet, two major cities in Bangladesh. The study utilized random sampling to select participants who were willing to voluntarily participate in the research. The sample size was determined using a formula based on a 95% confidence interval, with 350 participants ultimately included in the study. Data were collected through a semi-structured questionnaire, translated from English to Bangla and pre-tested for accuracy. The finalized questionnaire incorporated feedback from the pre-testing phase and underwent quality checks during data collection. The collected data were analysed using the Statistical Package for Social Science (SPSS) version 25.0. Descriptive statistics, including tables and graphs, were used to present the socio-demographic distribution of respondents, dietary habits, supplement intake, co-morbidities, and associations between dietary habits and COVID-19 infection. The chi-square test was employed to explore associations between categorical variables, with a significance level set at <0.05. Results: Prevalence od co morbidities is 39.4%. Socio-demographic findings revealed that 61.4% of respondents were female, predominantly aged 18-30 years (74%). Most were Muslim (84%) and resided in Sylhet (63%). Occupation-wise, 52.8% were private employees, and 10.5% were businessmen. Co-morbidity analysis showed 60.6% had no listed co-morbidities, while 4.6% reported asthma, and 5.1% high blood pressure. Dietary habits following COVID-19 awareness showed variations, with 37% having regular lunch, 36% regular dinner, and 27% regular breakfast. Street food consumption ranged from 3.7% regularly to 55.1% sometimes. Fast food consumption was reported by 3.1% regularly and 60.9% sometimes. Vegetable intake was associated with co-morbidities (p < 0.05), while street food, fast food, and weight changes showed no significant associations. Conclusion: The study highlights diverse post-COVID-19 dietary habits, with some individuals avoiding street and fast food. Significant associations between vegetable intake and co-morbidities emphasize the importance of dietary choices in health outcomes. These findings contribute to a better understanding of the pandemic's impact on dietary behaviors and health awareness in Bangladesh. Keywords: COVID-19, pandemic, dietary habit, Adults, Bangladesh

articles.title: Modality matters: differences in abstract and social memory skills in children from high-stress and low-stress environments
articles.authors: Gabriele Paone, Arran J. Davis, Emma Cohen
articles.abstract: Although adverse ontogenetic environments are associated with potential impairments in children's memory, recent research suggests that individuals can develop specialised skills to navigate such settings. We conducted two studies on short-term memory (STM) and working memory (WM) among 404 children (191 females, Mage = 8.28 years, SDage =1.49 years) from three environments in Naples, Italy: two identified as “high-stress” (Scampia and Roma camp) and one “lower-stress” (Pozzuoli). In Study 1, conventional abstract stimuli were used to assess STM and WM. Compared to the Pozzuoli control group, Roma children exhibited significantly lower scores, while Scampia children performed similarly. In Study 2, which used social stimuli, Roma children showed similar STM but lower WM performance compared to Pozzuoli children, while Scampia children outperformed Pozzuoli peers in both STM and WM. These findings highlight the complexity of memory development, supporting the idea that memory function can adapt to environmental challenges during development, and that impairments only partially define the experiences of children in these contexts.

articles.title: Fallacies of residential self-selection bias in travel behavior research: a reconceptualization
articles.authors: Fajle Rabbi Ashik, Christopher Paul Barrington-Leigh, Kevin Manaugh
articles.abstract: Residential self-selection (RSS) is widely recognized as a source of bias in studies of how the built environment affects travel behavior. The standard remedy is to control for individual travel attitudes, residential preferences, and sociodemographic characteristics. We show, however, that this approach misidentifies the nature of the bias and can worsen it. Using Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) to represent the causal structure of the travel behavior data-generating process, we demonstrate that RSS bias arises primarily not from confounding but from a form of selection bias. Because travel behavior is observed only at people’s chosen residential locations, and because that housing choice is jointly determined by individual characteristics and built environment attributes, the analysis is implicitly conditioned on a common outcome — a “collider” — of these two factors. Conditioning on a collider creates rather than removes spurious associations. Contrary to conventional practice, additionally controlling for travel attitude and residential preference typically amplifies the bias. We identify the appropriate covariate adjustment strategy and characterize two estimands of interest: the average causal effect and the net treatment difference. Our reconceptualization restores the built environment’s role in shaping travel behavior and has direct implications for transport policy analysis.
