articles.title: Patient-Reported Complexity in the Home Care: Italian Cross-Cultural Adaptation of the COMID-P Instrument
articles.authors: Sara Levati, Jessica Demarchi, Chiara barchielli, Catherine Ludwig, Catherine Busnel
articles.abstract: Background: Complexity in home care is increasingly understood as a multidimensional construct shaped by the interaction of clinical, psychosocial, environmental, and organizational factors, requiring assessment approaches that incorporate both professional and patient perspectives. Aim: To translate and cross-culturally adapt the French COMID-P into Italian to enable its application in Italy and Italian-speaking region of Switzerland. Methods: Following established guidelines, the instrument underwent forward translation, synthesis with cross-regional harmonization, back-translation, and expert review evaluation. Content validity was evaluated using I-CVI and S-CVI/Ave (4-point Likert scale) for clarity and relevance. Clarity was further assessed among nurses (n = 60) and patients (n = 9), with iterative revisions based on feedback. Results: Content validity was high (I-CVI 0.5-1 clarity; 0.75-1 relevance; S-CVI/Ave 0.92 and 0.95). Most items required no or minor refinement. All items met clarity criteria among nurses, while few patient-identified issues led to targeted terminology revisions, resulting in the final Italian version (COMID-Pi). Conclusion: The COMID-Pi demonstrates linguistic and cross-cultural adequacy, and it can now be used as PROM for assessing care complexity in Italian-speaking home care contexts.

articles.title: Kommunale Bürgerumfrage 2022
articles.authors: Stadt Leipzig, Amt für Statistik und Wahlen
articles.abstract: Bei der Kommunalen Bürgerumfrage handelt es sich um eine Mehrthemenbefragung (Omnibusbefragung). Die inhaltlichen Schwerpunkte ergeben sich aus den Informations­bedürfnissen der Stadtverwaltung. Der vorliegende Ergebnisbericht wertet ausgewählte Fragestellungen der Kommunalen Bürgerumfrage 2022 grafisch und textlich aus.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Police officer self-selection, not training, accounts for reported effects of procedural justice reform
articles.abstract: Procedural justice (PJ) training is a widely adopted police reform intervention, yet rigorous causal evidence of positive behavioral effects remains scarce. Most studies document null or mixed effects, and many rely on indirect outcomes rather than direct measures of officer behavior in the field. The strongest evidence for the effectiveness of PJ training comes from Camp et al. (2024), who provide sophisticated behavioral measurement at scale using a quasi-experimental design and body-worn camera footage-as-data. They report that officers increase PJ communication post-training. I demonstrate that these findings are attributable to officer selection into training sessions, not training itself. Reanalyzing the authors' replication data, I show that the 64.2% noncompliance with assigned training dates across the eight-month training broke the randomization. Earlier-trained officers had significantly higher pre-training PJ communication and accumulated disproportionately more post-training stops. The authors' findings of training efficacy are mechanically inflated. Once corrected for selection, the evidence no longer supports a positive effect of PJ training and reveals that officers may have been less likely to greet community members post-training. I outline research design practices that support credible causal inference in training evaluations.

articles.title: Refugee Origin and Public Attitudes toward Immigration after Russia’s Invasion of Ukraine: A Survey Experiment
articles.authors: Luise Salte, Tevfik Murat Yildirim, Liv Sunnercrantz, Carlo Michael Knotz, Thomas Sattich, Ragnhild Johanne sjurseike, Hande Eslen-Ziya
articles.abstract: Major geopolitical events can reshape how citizens think about immigration by making some migrant groups more salient and more sympathetic than others. Russia’s invasion of Ukraine created one of the largest refugee movements in Europe in recent decades and brought questions of forced migration, humanitarian obligation, and refugee reception to the center of public debate. We examine whether exposure to this crisis affected broader attitudes toward immigration. Using a pre-registered survey experiment conducted in Norway and the United Kingdom shortly after the invasion, we randomly assigned respondents to a prime about people fleeing the war in Ukraine, a prime about people fleeing conflict in the Middle East, or a control condition. Respondents then answered a battery of questions measuring attitudes toward immigration. We find that respondents exposed to the Ukraine prime expressed more positive immigration attitudes than those in the other experimental groups. This effect is especially visible for evaluations of immigrants’ cultural and societal contributions, while differences are weaker for economic evaluations. The findings suggest that major international crises can influence immigration attitudes, but that these effects depend on the perceived origin and social meaning of the refugee group in question. The study contributes to research on immigration attitudes, refugee reception, and the role of salient political events in shaping public opinion.

articles.title: Against Methodological Anthropocentrism: A Case for Artificial Reflexivity in Qualitative Inquiry
articles.abstract: Jowsey et al. (2025) argue that generative artificial intelligence should be rejected for reflexive qualitative research because it cannot make meaning, because qualitative inquiry is a distinctly human practice, and because the political economy of contemporary AI is environmentally and socially harmful. This paper takes the strongest possible counterposition. The central problem with categorical rejection is not that it underestimates current models in a merely practical sense; it is that it treats one historically local form of intelligence — human, embodied, biographical, socially recognized intelligence — as the only form that could ever count as interpretive or reflexive. That is a methodological version of anthropocentrism. If reflexivity is understood not as possession of a specifically human essence but as the disciplined capacity to model how one's priors, standpoint, procedures, and effects shape interpretation, then the question shifts. The relevant issue is no longer whether AI is human enough, but whether it can instantiate a different, alien form of recursive interpretive intelligence. Recent frontier systems already exhibit engineered forms of self-critique, revision, uncertainty signaling, long-context comparison, and auditable reasoning. Empirical studies show that large language models can support or outperform humans on some context-sensitive coding and annotation tasks. These findings do not erase serious limits: current systems lack lived stake, durable social accountability, stable metacognition, and full embodiment, and the infrastructure of AI raises real labor and environmental concerns. But those problems justify governance and methodological restraint, not a species veto. The better question is not whether AI can ever do qualitative inquiry, but what kinds of machine reflexivity are methodologically legitimate and under what conditions.

articles.title: Directional Bias in Electoral Polls: A Standardized Framework and the Introduction of the Matute Unit
articles.abstract: Electoral polls are commonly evaluated by comparing their estimates with observed election outcomes. However, observed discrepancies may arise from multiple sources, including sampling variability, methodological decisions, and systematic differences across polling organizations. This study introduces a standardized framework to assess directional deviations in electoral polling, focusing on pro-government bias. A metric, termed the Pro-Government Deviation Index (PGDI), is proposed, based on standardized polling errors relative to the distribution of errors across pollsters. Additionally, a rescaled unit of measurement, the Matute (Mt), is introduced to facilitate substantive interpretation of deviations by anchoring them to the empirical behavior of a reference pollster. Using Spanish electoral data, results indicate that the Centro de Investigaciones Sociológicas (CIS) exhibits a consistently higher pro-government deviation compared to other polling organizations, with a large effect size. The findings also reveal a broader polarization in house effects across pollsters. The proposed framework provides a transparent and interpretable approach for comparing polling performance and detecting systematic directional biases.

articles.title: Running Virtual Reality Experiments Online: A Brief Introduction and Tutorial
articles.authors: Levi Kumle, Alfie Brazier, Joel Kovoor, Johannes Stefan Keil, Anna C Nobre, Dejan Draschkow
articles.abstract: Achieving meaningful and representative findings in cognitive/behavioural research requires both externally valid experimental settings and representative participant samples. Virtual Reality (VR) has become a powerful tool for enhancing realism while maintaining control of experimental environments. Simultaneously, online testing broadens and diversifies samples. Combining these approaches – running VR experiments online – is compelling but remains challenging due to incompatibilities between tools used in in lab-based VR studies vs. online testing. This paper offers a practical solution to bridge this gap. We provide a brief overview of current approaches to online VR experimentation and offer a step-by-step tutorial for converting lab-based VR studies into remote deployments using Unity and Steam. Further, we share an open-source onlineVR-toolbox – including code, example workflows, and practical Notebooks – to facilitate critical processes such as secure data transfer and participant management. To validate this approach, we present a proof-of-concept case study replicating a well-established memory effect. Our results demonstrate that remote data collection via Steam is technically feasible, and yields results consistent with laboratory-based findings. Together, our tutorial, tools, and case study show that online VR studies are possible and practical, opening the door to more scalable, inclusive, and externally valid behavioural science.

articles.title: ‘It Felt Like All My Friendship Issues Suddenly Made Sense’: The Friendship Experiences of Late-Diagnosed Autistic Women
articles.abstract: Research shows autistic people may struggle to form and maintain friendships and that these challenges may be especially seen in autistic women and girls. A qualitative retrospective design was adopted to investigate late-diagnosed autistic women’s experiences of making/maintaining friendships, and how receiving a diagnosis in adulthood impacted these experiences. Seven autistic women from the UK, diagnosed over the age of 18, participated in semi-structured interviews, analysed using reflexive thematic analysis. Women felt their autistic characteristics, and differing friendship wants created challenges, and they reported receiving limited support with their friendships growing up. However, receiving a diagnosis made it possible for women to accept and comprehend themselves and their past friendship experiences, including recognising a preference for having autistic friends prior to diagnosis. For many, receiving a diagnosis improved friendships, and every woman wished they had been diagnosed earlier, acknowledging that the process of self-acceptance could be distressing later in life. Practical implications for support are discussed.

articles.title: A significant minority of Dutch gambling adverts on social media illegally targeted and likely reached under-24s: Assessing compliance using Meta’s advertising repository mandated by the EU Digital Services Act
articles.abstract: Background and aims Many countries have legalised gambling, negatively impacting public health. Stakeholders are particularly concerned about online gambling advertising harming young adults. The Netherlands has legalised offline and online gambling, but subsequently introduced restrictions on advertising. Specifically, advertisements must not target young adults aged between 18 and 23. The EU Digital Services Act requires major social media platforms to publish databases of all paid adverts shown and disclose which demographic groups were the intended targets and how many users were eventually reached. We assessed whether Dutch gambling ads on social media illegally targeted under-24s. Methods We systematically collected recent advertisements (N = 277) published by both online and offline Dutch gambling licensees and associated demographic targeting and reach data using Meta's Ad Library. We then assessed whether the age range of users targeted by those ads included prohibited targets. Results The compliance rate was 92.7% for online gambling licensee ads, but only 70.2% for offline licensee ads. Discussion A significant minority of gambling ads illegally targeted Dutch young adults under 24. Whilst compliance is relatively high, building some confidence, the regulator must robustly enforce the rule against all licensees. Meta should adopt platform-level changes to reduce compliance friction. Conclusions Prohibiting gambling adverts from targeting vulnerable demographics using technology is a practicable policy. Legally mandated data access can facilitate public scrutiny of policy implementation, enhancing accountability. Other countries may benefit from emulating these Dutch gambling advertising restrictions and EU advertising transparency regulations, better protecting vulnerable consumer demographics and enabling local research.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Unpleasant mind, deactivated body – A distinct somatic signature of boredom through bodily sensation mapping
articles.authors: Matthias Hartmann, Wanja Wolff, Corinna Martarelli
articles.abstract: Boredom is a common yet complex state linked to low arousal and negative valence, but its bodily signature remains poorly understood. We used Bodily Sensation Mapping (BSM)—a self-report technique that visualizes where and how emotions are felt in the body—to investigate boredom’s somatic pattern. In an online study, 292 participants colored body silhouettes to indicate regions of increased or decreased activation and pleasantness for boredom, anger, anxiety, happiness, sadness, and curiosity. When immersed in boredom, participants reported a localized unpleasantness in the head alongside mild bodily deactivation. Linear discriminant analysis confirmed that this somatotopic valence/activity-pattern was distinct from other emotions, supporting the idea that boredom is a unique state with characteristic arousal and valence experiences. Despite this consistent group-level pattern, individual differences emerged with regards to valence sensations in the body, yet these were not linked with boredom proneness and adaptive dealing with boredom. In contrast, these traits variables correlated with distinct body and head activity patterns, particularly among individuals with heightened interoceptive accuracy. Our results underscore the utility of BSM for nuanced mapping of complex emotional states and advance our understanding of how bodily sensations interact with trait factors to shape the subjective experience of boredom.

articles.title: Young carers and educational attainment in late adolescence: a nationally representative analysis of post-16 education in England
articles.authors: Alejandra Letelier, Anne McMunn, Andy McGowan, Krista Cartlidge, Rebecca Lacey
articles.abstract: Introduction: Young carers, defined as individuals <18 years providing unpaid care for someone with disabilities, health conditions, or addiction, are a frequently overlooked group. In the UK, around 12% of young people report caring responsibilities, yet no national studies have examined their post-16 educational attainment outcomes. This study assessed associations between young caring and attainment at Key Stage 5 (age 16-18 years) in England and whether these varied by sociodemographic factors and care characteristics. Methods: Data were drawn from Understanding Society linked to the National Pupil Database in England (2009–2018). The analytic sample included 3,306 adolescents aged 17–18 years. Caring status was self-reported; attainment outcomes were obtained from administrative records. Regression models adjusted for demographic and socioeconomic factors. Additional analyses examined care recipient and care intensity plus modification by gender, ethnicity and socioeconomic position. Results: Young carers had lower educational attainment than their peers. Compared with non-carers, they obtained fewer qualifications (A-Level and equivalent passes, IRR = 0.88, 95% CI 0.79–0.98), were less likely to achieve two or more A-Levels at grades A*–E (OR = 0.67, 95% CI 0.48–0.93) and scored lower on academic and A-Level point totals. In addition, they were also more likely to follow a vocational pathway (OR = 1.40, 95% CI 1.03–1.92). Attainment gaps were larger among boys and higher care intensity (≥10 hours/week). Conclusions: Young carers face persistent educational attainment inequalities at the end of compulsory education, particularly those providing intensive care and boys, highlighting the need for early identification and targeted support.

articles.title: Quantifying the reproductive labor of pregnancy and pregnancy loss in the demographic study of population health
articles.abstract: Pregnancy loss remains a neglected topic within demography, overlooking the significant costs of women’s reproductive labor globally. Using data from Demographic and Health Survey reproductive calendars across 65 countries, this analysis moves beyond live birth to describe the prevalence of pregnancy loss that women across countries experience. The analysis first quantifies the substantial number of person-months that women spend being pregnant in both pregnancies that end in live birth and those that do not. It then calculates a measure of maternal cumulative prevalence of pregnancy loss, demonstrating that women’s experience of pregnancy loss is common worldwide. Next, following the methodology of the Total Fertility Rate (TFR), the analysis calculates a parallel measure called the Total Pregnancy Rate (TPR) to show the average number of pregnancies a woman would experience if she survived through the end of the reproductive years and experienced at each age the period age- specific pregnancy rates. The TPR ranges from 4% to 115% larger than the TFR across countries, with an average of 16% larger. Given that pregnancy loss is known to be undermeasured in many places, these are likely lower bound magnitudes. Finally, cross-country variation in pregnancy loss shows no discernable relationship with potential determinants of pregnancy loss such as abortion incidence rates, per capita health expenditure, rates of anemia prevalence, or TFR, demonstrating the limits of existing fertility frameworks. Counting and considering women’s reproductive labor in the form of pregnancy, whether it ends in a live birth or not, is crucial to understanding women’s and population health, and for gaining a more complete picture of the underlying costs of demographic measures of fertility.

articles.title: The Limits of BPE Tokenization in Polish: Segmentation-Flexional Forms, Grammatical Anchoring, and First-Person Stability in Inflectional Language Models
articles.abstract: This article analyzes BPE tokenization in Polish as a test case for a broader problem: the limits of statistical segmentation in an inflectional language. The starting point is the question of whether, and to what extent, frequency-based tokenization preserves units that are relevant for the linguistic description of Polish: orthographic form, the segmentation-phonemic level, syllable structure, logotomes, prefixes, derivational bases, inflectional endings, grammatical form, and the position of the speaking subject. The empirical material includes the tokenization of diagnostic words, a simple children’s text, selected forms from the Preamble to the Constitution of the Republic of Poland, word-family tests, and examples containing Polish diacritics and nasal vowels. The analysis shows that BPE tokenizers may locally produce segments that coincide with syllabic, logotomic, or morphologically interpretable divisions, but they remain fundamentally dependent on the frequency of written forms. They do not systematically move from orthographic representation to the segmentation-phonemic level or to context-dependent phonetic realization. The results also indicate that BPE stabilizes not grammatical categories themselves, but frequent surface fragments of their exponents. Segments such as -y, -ich, -ni, or -ć may appear as stable tokens or parts of tokens, but this does not amount to preserving the full grammatical form. A form such as ustanawiamy is not merely a sequence ending in -y; it is a verbal form anchored in conjugation, person, number, tense, mood, and aspect. The article develops the concept of grammatical form anchoring, arguing that in Polish a form may not only encode syntactic relations, but also establish the position of the speaking subject. In forms such as poszłam, zrobiłam, or byłam, the grammatical “I” need not be expressed by a separate pronoun, because it is anchored in inflection. In interaction with AI, this reveals an additional problem: a language model does not possess its own stable grammatical “I”, but reconstructs it contextually, often mirroring the user’s forms or shifting grammatical gender. I propose treating Rocławski’s segmentation-flexional forms not as a description of how BPE operates, but as a diagnostic framework for assessing the quality of tokenization boundaries. The findings lead to the research hypothesis that more stable modeling of Polish may require several layers: sublexical stabilization inspired by Rocławski’s system, anchoring grammatical form in the inflectional system, representing sentence patterns and verbal valency structure, and maintaining the position of the grammatical “I” in dialogue. From this perspective, the problem of Polish AI is not reducible to reducing the number of tokens. Rather, it concerns maintaining relations between orthographic representation, word form, grammatical function, sentence position, and the position of the speaking subject.

articles.title: Which Businesses Answer Surveys? Evidence from Dutch Administrative Data
articles.abstract: I leverage a unique administrative register covering the universe of establishments in the Netherlands to examine how characteristics differ between establishments that do and do not respond to business surveys. Only 19% of Dutch establishments responded to regional business surveys in 2022. Responsive establishments employ fewer people, and exhibit higher parttime employment rates, than unresponsive establishments. Sectoral response rates vary by up to 32 percentage points. Enterprises registered to residential addresses comprise over 70% of establishments, yet exhibit response rates 18 percentage points lower than an average office. However, controlling for contact probability reveals that the majority of sectoral and occupational variation in response rates can be traced back to differences in contact probability rather than responsiveness. These findings highlight challenges in the representativeness and generalizability of business survey data, as well as opportunities to improve the design of business surveys.

articles.title: The Effect of a Woman-Friendly Occupation on Employment: U.S. Postmasters Before World War II
articles.abstract: This paper studies U.S. postmasters in the early twentieth century, a rare woman-friendly occupation at a time when women’s employment opportunities were highly constrained. Using a novel dataset linking postmaster appointments to census records, I show that these well-paid, flexible positions attracted educated and qualified White women into the labor force. However, exploiting exogenous variation in reappointment probabilities through an RD design, I find that many women exited the labor force once their appointments ended. Few transitioned into other skilled occupations, accounting for only one-third of the 24 pp. employment loss. The results are consistent with a reservation-wage mechanism: women who secured alternative full-time skilled jobs with wages comparable to postmasters remained employed, while others stopped working. The scarcity of jobs similar to postmasters likely limited the long-term gains of this otherwise advantageous occupation.

articles.title: Governing online hate: The construction of “hateful ideology” in TikTok’s platform governance from 2018 to 2025
articles.abstract: Social media platforms’ understanding of online hate is itself a governance choice with normative and political implications. This study examines the evolution of TikTok’s hate speech governance from 2018 to 2025, focusing on how the platform has conceptualized and deployed the governance concept of “hateful ideology” across its community guidelines, news releases, and transparency reports. Applying longitudinal document analysis (N=132), this study identified two major shifts in the platform’s hate speech policy: a 2020 conceptual shift that restructured hate speech around a four-category framework and a 2023 structural shift that reconfigured policy enforcement around a tiered visibility framework, with “hateful ideology” becoming a central organizing concept. TikTok’s present conception of hateful ideology combines a cognitivist framing of ideology (as belief systems) and a practice-based enforcement logic—a conceptual layering that produces interpretive discretion. The findings suggest that TikTok has primarily configured its concept of “hateful ideology” as a legitimation device calibrated to regulatory contexts, rather than a stable governance principle.

articles.title: Housing Prices and English Period Fertility
articles.abstract: The aim of the study is to produce an estimate of the impact of house prices on English and Welsh period fertility during 2010-23, a period in which there were large declines in period fertility, large changes in house prices and considerable heterogeneity in regional variation of house prices. It uses individual longitudinal data from Understanding Society (the first 14 waves), and it identifies impacts of real house prices on age-specific birth probabilities from variation in changes over time in prices within each region of residence. The estimates imply relatively large negative impacts of real house prices on age-specific birth probabilities. They can be interpreted as causal effects if two conditions hold: (1) there are no omitted time-varying regional variables correlated with house prices and fertility; and (2) women do not move to regions of lower prices in anticipation of future pregnancy. It contributes to the literature by using data on individual women rather than area aggregates. It also employs birth registration data to cross-validate the predictions of the Total Fertility Rate (TFR) and mean age at childbirth from the estimated model.

articles.title: From Bread to Bullets: Sectoral Differences in how Aid Changes the Geography of Conflict
articles.abstract: How does humanitarian aid influence armed conflict dynamics, and how do its effects vary across sectors? This paper analyzes geocoded humanitarian projects and conflict events across Africa (2000–2024) to examine how sector-specific aid shapes territorial control and armed clashes. I argue that aid alters the strategic value of locations through two mechanisms: lootability (material extractability) and visibility (governance signaling). Using matched panel models and spatial grid-cell data, I compare conflict trends across areas with varying levels of aid exposure. Results show that aid increases territorial contestation and clashes. Food and water drive regional violence linked to access competition. Lootable aid produces universal competition, while governance-intensive aid destabilizes rebel-held zones. Violence against aid workers remains limited and largely opportunistic.

articles.title: The Cooperative Scope of Local Gods: Punishment, Omniscience, and Distant Cooperation among the Sihanaka of Madagascar
articles.authors: Anders Norge, Daniel Major-Smith, Benjamin Grant Purzycki
articles.abstract: Do supernatural beliefs in punishment and omniscience promote cooperation among distant unrelated individuals? Previous cross-cultural research – predominantly among “moralistic” religious traditions – suggests it might, although numerous open questions remain. Foremost among these are: “Do these results replicate among local religious traditions?” and “Is the relationship causal?” Here, we take a causally-informed approach to explore these questions among the Sihanaka from Madagascar. Our research question is: Do punishment and omniscience beliefs about a local god - zazavavindrano, an aquatic deity - promote cooperation towards co-religionists of this tradition? We assessed cooperation using Random Allocation and Dictator Games. Bayesian regression models were used, adjusting for plausible confounding factors, with g-computation methods to estimate our counterfactual causal effects of interest (i.e., the joint effect of zazavavindrano punishment and omniscience beliefs on cooperation with distant co-religionists). Across all games, conditions, and confounding assumptions, we found little evidence for a relationship between these zazavavindrano beliefs and cooperation. While zazavavindrano beliefs may not promote cooperation with unknown distant co-religionists, whether these beliefs facilitate cooperation in other domains – such as ecological management of wetlands, which is of greater concern to zazavavindrano than moral behaviour towards strangers – is an interesting open question for future research.

articles.title: An Astonishing Turn in the Attribution Debate on the Law of Comparative Advantage
articles.abstract: The law of comparative advantage cannot be attributed to David Ricardo, James Mill, Robert Torrens or John Stuart Mill. Their writings, much like those of Adam Smith, strictly adhere to the classical rule for specialization, which asserts that one should acquire commodities abroad whenever offered cheaper than it would cost to produce them locally. They all viewed the cheaper price of foreign commodities as the logical starting point and condicio sine qua non of most exchanges between countries. Furthermore, the popular narrative about Ricardo’s alleged formulation of an alternative rule for specialization – the law or principle of comparative advantage – results from a misinterpretation of the well-known numerical example presented in Chapter 7 of his Principles of Political Economy and Taxation. John Stuart Mill is largely responsible for this misinterpretation; he obviously deserves no recognition or praise for that.

articles.title: Illicit Financial Outflows and Domestic Linkage Disruption in Nigeria
articles.abstract: Illicit financial outflows remain a major constraint on Nigeria’s development, yet much of the literature focuses on their causes and scale rather than their effects on domestic economic linkages. This paper examines illicit financial outflows through the capital flight channel, using residual-method capital flight estimates as an empirical proxy for unrecorded outward resource movements. The paper develops an Economic Linkages Framework to explain how capital flight disrupts the channels through which domestic investment generates multiplier effects, employment, fiscal capacity, supply-chain deepening, and productivity spillovers. Drawing on Hirschman’s (1958) linkage theory, Keynesian multiplier analysis, and endogenous growth theory, it introduces a nine-type typology of domestic linkages covering production, financial, fiscal, labour market, consumption, technology, trade-external, institutional, and spatial-territorial dimensions. The empirical analysis is illustrative rather than causal, given the small sample of 26 annual observations. OLS results show that unemployment is strongly and negatively associated with GDP growth (R² = 0.574), suggesting that the labour market channel is the most visible linkage pathway in the available data. The direct and squared capital flight terms are not statistically significant, so the non-linear linkage disruption hypothesis remains suggestive rather than confirmed. The paper contributes a conceptual framework for analysing illicit financial outflows as a process of domestic linkage disruption and structural fragmentation, with implications for capital retention, employment, fiscal capacity, and institutional reform in Nigeria and comparable resource-dependent economies.

articles.title: Does Carbon Pricing Outperform Command-and-Control Regulation? Firm-Level Evidence from Korea’s Dual Regulatory Framework
articles.abstract: This study exploits South Korea's unique dual-policy framework to evaluate the comparative effects of carbon pricing and command-and-control regulation on firm-level environmental performance. Using a difference-in-differences design with firm-level panel data from 2011 to 2022, I compare outcomes between firms regulated under a command-and-control program (Target Management System, TMS) and those subject to a market-based carbon pricing mechanism (Emissions Trading Scheme, ETS). The results show that ETS-regulated firms reduced energy use by approximately 5.8% to 8.8% and carbon emissions by 7.3% to 8.5% across model specifications. However, the effects on carbon intensity were inconsistent. Event-study analyses suggest that these differing effects are driven by the heterogeneous timing of firm responses: immediate but short-lived reductions in energy use, persistent declines in carbon emissions, and gradual improvements in emissions efficiency. Phase-specific estimates further indicate that more market-oriented ETS phases were associated with stronger reductions in carbon emissions and intensity, underscoring the role of incentive-based policy design in enhancing environmental outcomes.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Reverse-engineering is not enough for inter-group transmission of novel palaeolithic knapping techniques
articles.authors: Vincent Niochet, Morgan Roussel, Krist Vaesen, Alexander Verpoorte, Marie Soressi
articles.abstract: Identifying evidence of cultural transmission from archaeological artefacts is essential for reconstructing the dynamics of social interactions during prehistory. Although technological similarities among overlapping archaeological cultures are often interpreted as indicators of direct contact, the degree to which social interaction is necessary for the diffusion of knapping technologies remains debated. Transmission of technologies might, in some cases, result from the reverse-engineering of discarded stone artefacts. Here, we experimentally tested the library of stones hypothesis, which posits that even an unfamiliar knapping technique can be identified solely through observation of its resulting artefacts. To evaluate this, 34 experienced knappers participated in an experiment in which they were asked to identify the knapping technique used to produce three experimental sets of stone artefacts, each made with a distinct technique. Participants generally failed to reverse-engineer techniques with which they were unfamiliar. Successful reverse-engineering was primarily driven by prior practical experience of the technique and diversity of technological repertoire. These results suggest that the cultural transmission of technological innovations likely required direct social learning mechanisms to occur between groups. Experimental approaches of this kind offer valuable insights into the processes underlying cultural transmission and provide a promising framework for reconstructing inter-group interaction during prehistory.

articles.title: Better futures: Supported accommodation for unaccompanied children and young people experiencing homelessness
articles.authors: Catherine Robinson, Carmel Hobbs, Deborah Batterham, Joel McGregor, Wendy Stone, Megan Robinson
articles.abstract: This research project examines the effectiveness of Australia’s supported accommodation services in meeting the needs of unaccompanied children and young people aged 12–24. It proposes principles to guide policy and practice toward a better system. Unaccompanied children and young people experiencing homelessness are at risk of significant lifelong negative outcomes. Supported accommodation services are a critical response to homelessness; however, these services do not always meet this cohort’s needs. A strengthened system of care is required to better align services to unaccompanied young people’s needs.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: From Lombroso to Cognitive Immunity: Integrating Classical Criminological Theory with the Integrated Bio-Ethical Literacy (IBL) Model — The Impulse Gap, Deterrence Deficit, and Regulatory Realism as Theoretical Contributions to the Primary Prevention of Child Sexual Offences
articles.abstract: Classical criminology has, over a century and a half, produced increasingly sophisticated explanations of why human beings commit acts of sexual violence against children — from Lombroso's constitutional positivism to Sutherland's sociology of learned conduct to Bandura's account of moral disengagement. Each stage has advanced the field. None has produced a systematic, evidence-based architecture for primary prevention: a mechanism for rendering such offending structurally less probable before it occurs. That is the gap this paper addresses. Three theoretical constructs are advanced here — the Impulse Gap, the Deterrence Deficit, and Regulatory Realism — each grounded in existing criminological literature but applied, for the first time as a synthesized framework, to the specific preventive challenge posed by child sexual offending in India. The Impulse Gap identifies the explanatory space within Sutherland's Differential Association framework — partially addressed but not resolved by Gottfredson and Hirschi's self-control theory — where opportunistic, neurobiologically driven offending escapes both social learning explanation and rational-actor deterrence. The Deterrence Deficit names what the empirical literature has long established but Indian child protection policy has not yet systematically absorbed: that severity-based punitive frameworks, however necessary, demonstrably fail to arrest offending trajectories when deployed without complementary preventive educational infrastructure. Regulatory Realism articulates the policy philosophy that follows from the developmental criminological tradition — from Sutherland through Wikström — that the conditions of childhood are not fixed and that their educational regulation is a legitimate and underutilized instrument of child protection. The empirical component of this inquiry, drawn from N=108 stakeholders across Bhopal Division's five districts, provides ground-level validation for these constructs: among practitioners, the deterrent efficacy of police action achieved only a 56 per cent endorsement — a respondent-reported perception consistent with what Nagin (1998) and Doob and Webster (2003) have established about severity-based deterrence across jurisdictions. Together, these three constructs provide the criminological foundation for the Integrated Bio-Ethical Literacy (IBL) Model — not a departure from the discipline's intellectual tradition, but its extension into the domain of primary prevention that Lombroso raised but could not answer and that Sutherland opened but did not close.

articles.title: Ginagnotología: la producción estructural de ignorancia en la salud de las mujeres / Gynagnotology: The Structural Production of Ignorance in Women’s Health
articles.abstract: RESUMEN En las últimas décadas, la literatura científica ha documentado de forma consistente la presencia de sesgos de sexo y género en la producción y aplicación del conocimiento biomédico, generando lagunas relevantes en la salud de las mujeres. Estas desigualdades no pueden explicarse únicamente mediante sesgos individuales, errores metodológicos o falta de evidencia, sino que responden a dinámicas estructurales que producen y organizan ignorancia de manera sistemática. Este trabajo introduce y desarrolla el concepto de ginagnotología, propuesto por la autora, como un campo analítico dentro de la agnotología que examina cómo el sesgo de sexo y género opera como un mecanismo estructural de producción, organización y legitimación de la ignorancia en el sistema biomédico. A partir de este concepto, se propone un marco triádico que articula tres niveles analíticos interrelacionados: ginagnotología (nivel epistemológico), ginopia médica (nivel clínico) y morbilidad diferencial (nivel epidemiológico), permitiendo conectar la producción de ignorancia con sus manifestaciones en la práctica clínica y sus efectos en las desigualdades en salud. La ginagnotología ofrece así un marco conceptual para convertir la ignorancia estructurada en objeto de estudio crítico y orientar su transformación, abriendo vías hacia una biomedicina más rigurosa y equitativa. ABSTRACT In recent decades, scientific literature has consistently documented the presence of sex and gender biases in the production and application of biomedical knowledge, generating significant gaps in women’s health. These inequalities cannot be explained solely by individual biases, methodological errors, or lack of evidence, but rather reflect structural dynamics that systematically produce and organize ignorance. This paper introduces and develops the concept of gynagnotology, proposed by the author, as an analytical field within agnotology that examines how sex and gender bias operates as a structural mechanism for the production, organization, and legitimation of ignorance in the biomedical system. Building on this concept, a triadic framework is proposed that articulates three interrelated analytical levels: gynagnotology (epistemological level), medical gynopia (clinical level), and differential morbidity (epidemiological level), enabling the connection between the production of ignorance, its manifestation in clinical practice, and its effects on health inequalities. Gynagnotology thus provides a conceptual framework to turn structured ignorance into an object of critical inquiry and to guide its transformation, opening pathways toward a more rigorous and equitable biomedicine.

articles.title: Metodologi Penelitian Adaptif di Era Digital
articles.abstract: Buku ini berfokus pada pemahaman mendalam tentang konsep metodologi yang tidak hanya bersifat prosedural, tetapi juga reflektif dan inovatif. Pembaca akan diajak menelusuri bagaimana Artificial Intelligence (AI), digitalisasi data, dan konektivitas global telah mengubah lanskap penelitian modern. Salah satu gagasan penting yang diperkenalkan dalam buku ini adalah Model F.A.D.L.I (Formulate, Analyze, Design, and Improve) sebuah pendekatan penelitian dan pengembangan (Research and Development Model) yang dirancang untuk menuntun peneliti agar mampu merumuskan ide, menganalisis secara sistematis, mendesain solusi, dan melakukan perbaikan berkelanjutan. Model ini diharapkan dapat menjadi inspirasi dan rujukan baru bagi mahasiswa, dosen, serta peneliti yang ingin beradaptasi dengan era digital tanpa kehilangan esensi keilmuan.

articles.title: "I'd Rather Have Milk Tea": Reproductive Refusal, Platform Divergence, and the Gendered Architecture of Fertility Discourse on Chinese Social Media--An AI-Assisted Topic Modeling Study
articles.authors: Frank Tian-fang Ye, GAO Xiaozi, Mac Zewei Ma
articles.abstract: China's total fertility rate has fallen to approximately 0.98, the lowest since 1949, yet the discursive dimensions of this decline remain underexplored. This study examines how young Chinese women articulate fertility attitudes across two platforms with contrasting affordances: Weibo, oriented toward public debate, and Xiaohongshu (RedNote), oriented toward lifestyle and embodied experience. Using BERTopic neural topic modelling on 495,893 Weibo posts (2023 to 2025) and 222 viral Xiaohongshu posts, we identify 164 Weibo topics that we organise into seven meta-themes spanning reproductive refusal, structural constraints, and embodied experience. Temporal analysis reveals that reproductive autonomy anxiety dominated Weibo discourse for 31 consecutive months, while a "blind box" metaphor for childbearing rose steadily in prominence. Cross-platform comparison shows that the same generation processes fertility politically on Weibo, through feminist critique and structural analysis, but embodies it on Xiaohongshu through pregnancy, postpartum, and lifestyle frames. We argue that this divergence reflects the interaction of platform affordances, algorithmic curation, and content moderation rather than distinct populations. The persistence of reproductive refusal signals deep tensions between state pronatalism and young women's lived experience of inadequate support for motherhood.

articles.title: A Comparison of Process Tracing and Congruence Analysis for the Evaluation of a Population Health Intervention
articles.authors: Emma Carey, David Ogilvie, Kate Garrott, Richard Patterson, Olivia Alliott, Jenna Panter
articles.abstract: Mixed methods evaluations of population health interventions are often comprised of diverse components which must be integrated to provide holistic insight into effects and their causes. Theory-based methods can be used to support this integration, but research into how to select which method to use is limited. Traffic restriction schemes that limit motor vehicle access to the roads outside of schools in the UK have recently proliferated in the UK. We explore how two theory-based methods – process tracing (theory testing) and congruence analysis – could be used to evaluate such schemes. We use hypothetical data to illustrate how each method could be applied. In our case, congruence analysis was more suitable because we sought to evaluate multiple possible pathways by which schemes might influence travel. Theory-testing process tracing would be more suited to research with narrower hypotheses. Combining approaches may also help to both identify and examine key causal pathways.
